02 How We Do Meetings at Scalable - YouTube.mp4
Hey there! My name is Ryan Dice, CEO of the Scalable Company. This is my co-founder, business partner, Richard Lindner, who's the president. He is the grown-up, which isn't saying much, but... Basically, what we're going to talk about is kind of how we do meetings at the Scalable Company, or maybe said another way, how we don't do them. We've got a lot of regularly scheduled meetings here. We do our quarterly sprint planning meetings as a leadership team. We do monthly all-hands where we're kind of doing the check-ins. I know teams, different teams are going to have their regularly scheduled team meetings. But aside from those meetings that are the regularly scheduled, like planning or 'What's going on? What are the other types of meetings that we do here?
And really, how should meetings happen or not? Kind of talk me through that. Yeah, that's a great question and something that I think can be a giant both time and resource waster in just meetings. So for us, outside of those scheduled meetings, really when we're looking at what other meetings should exist or could exist, one, I think there's a Blue Ocean meeting. So, the blue ocean meaning there is a giant opportunity that we need to all round up and meet about, because, and this is the important kind of disclaimer here, because if we don't take advantage of this now, we may not be able to receive as much benefit later. Taking advantage of this now means we'd have to change our stated goals or plans.
We can't both take advantage of this new opportunity and continue with our agreed upon plan. So the opportunity has to be so big you've got to feel like, at least, if you're going to call basically a blue ocean meeting, I declare a 'blution' meeting. You're going to make that public declaration. You got to feel like this is a big enough thing. That it's worthy of changing. The goals that we have. Have said so. Uh, what if it's not? What do you do if they're like, you know, it's important, but it's it's not quite that. What then? That's a backlog. Right. So if an opportunity is there, really—again, I think the way to judge that isn't just on the results. Maybe your success metrics for this quarter.
But if you're going to call a blue ocean, that's usually going to expedite your three-year plan. Right, it's it that we're talking about something that could take you, your year two, and make it the where you end up— your year one or your year three. We're gonna cut 12 months off of that and it's gonna be our year two. So it needs to be a giant opportunity and one that cannot really wait to be capitalized on. Or the benefits would go down dramatically. So, if you feel like it's a big opportunity, but it's not at that blue ocean level, then I know for us, you know, we keep what we call the B &O list. Bottlenecks and opportunities. I believe that at least, you know, if you're here, your team should also have a B &O list.
Basically, that would just be, let's add this to the opportunities list. We can chat about it at the team meeting. Maybe it gets backlogged and it gets kind of percolated up to the quarterly sprint plan. Backlog it there, backlog. In your key initiatives board. So somewhere where you're not going to forget about it. Right. Because it's still an opportunity. It just isn't something that's going to change the course of where you're at now. Kind of the... um. So that's the positive side, right? So that's the opportunistic side. But what about the other side, right? What if something happens that isn't positive, right? And we would call this threat a black swan. So you're looking at a black swan meeting. So something very, very bad is happening.
And if we don't alter our course, it's not just that we likely won't. Hit our stated goals, but we may go well backwards. Something the business has fundamentally changed— we're not in the business, but maybe environmentally right. Like, maybe a global pandemic would be a reason that we need to round up something that happened outside of our control that is changing everything. So we need to say, what does this mean for our business? And do we have any reason to believe that we can keep going forward with the stated plans? Like, stuff changes outside of our control. Sometimes it's globally, sometimes it's in our... industry, sometimes it's in just our business or our sector. So those things happen, and we have to have a way to wave the flag and to call that in the same way.
You know, I'm calling a black swan. Like, we have got to get together. It is a giant threat. But if it's not, because I mean, that's a high bar, right? Yeah. And I think that, then again, we're talking about when is it appropriate to like... I'm going to call a meeting. We're going to get a lot of people together because anytime you call a meeting, you got to know it's expensive. Especially if you're calling a meeting of the leadership team. Right, yeah, we've got to get everybody here. Maybe get the whole team here because, again, teams are meeting every single week generally. So there's going to be an opportunity to discuss these things. So this is assuming you're going to call it an extra special meeting. It's got to be a big deal.
If not, because we're not saying that, hey— Look, if a problem arises, but it's not like this kind of world-shaking type thing, just ignore it. We're not saying that at all. We're saying... Discuss that at the... Already almost certainly already scheduled team meeting. You've got meetings. Yeah, right there. There should be a normal meeting rhythm and cadence. Yeah, but you got to think about this like if this were a train. If your company's a train, you're pulling that cord that just locks up the brakes and stops the train. That's one of these two meetings, right? We're saying either there's an opportunity or a threat that we need to reevaluate our plans. Apart from that... Pick it up at the team meeting because all teams should be meeting at least weekly.
Correct. Your team should be meeting at least weekly. I know we got a lot of teams here that do twice daily. So you can do it there. But if it's not a pull the cord kind of moment, then it's get it on the backlog, add it to the B &O list, talk about it. That's a particular bottleneck. Let's talk about it. The next team meeting. Correct. Okay. And your leadership team should be meeting weekly, right? So, I mean, if you're thinking about the number of meetings that happen, something comes up and even if you're unsure, I'll tell you: if you're unsure whether it's a blue ocean or a black swan, don't pull that cord. Don't stop the train. It should never be more than six.
Six days before you talk again at that level and just also confirming, like, 'Hey, I think this is an opportunity or I think this is a threat.' What do you think? But really putting it on that B &O list and talking about it at the next meeting. If you're unsure, that's the way to go about that. Yeah, and again, B &O list, that's the bottlenecks and opportunities list. It should exist at every single level of this company. So, if you're unclear about how do I access the B &O list for our team, make sure that you talk to your manager. We'll get that figured out. So we got kind of those red flag black swans. We've got the blue ocean, like, holy crap, this is great.
What else? What other times might it be appropriate to call a special meeting? So... I mean, there's a project meeting, right? And usually a project meeting. I don't know, it's not necessarily a special meeting; it's supporting a decision that's already been made. There's a fantastic book called Read This Before Our Next Meeting. It's kind of the stuff that we have adopted over the past five years here. Really. It talks about the concept of the modern meeting, right? The modern meeting supports a decision that's already been made. So if you're going to call a meeting. Typically and it's not one of those two then it's it's usually for one of two things. It's usually for clarification right or kickoff right if I need to kick off a project or a meeting like some initiative we've already greenlit.
I'm gonna bring all the parties together, we're gonna kick it off. It's it's kind of a launch event, which is fine. It's because it's about a project that's already been greenlit. It's not a diversion from the plan. It's moving forward on the plan. It's a... It's action. It's implementation. It's motion on a plan. Exactly. So that's kind of your kickoff. The only other two reasons to have a meeting about a decision that's already been made is clarity or conflict. That's it, right? I need more clarity. On the decision we already made, on the initiative we already greenlit, on the project that we said we were going to do. Either because I'm stuck, or there's more information and now I don't know. Or there's conflicts between two parties.
I think I should do it this way. You think you should do it that way. We've got to kick it up because at some point we cannot come to any kind of similar position. Yeah, and that's important. Very often, two people—or multiple people, teams—If they're in conflict. There will be a lot of back and forth communication. Right. And I'll tell you, that's when stuff gets lost in translation. Feelings can get hurt. Miscommunication occurs. The best thing that can happen and a really good use of meetings. Like again, in general, less meetings in general, try to not have meetings, but a great time to call a meeting. Is we are actively going down this path for this product project that's been approved. We got some conflict. We've got some uncertainty.
Conflict doesn't have to be like— we're throwing stuff at each other and you know, calling other names. It's just like, you know, there's a lack of clarity. Um, we don't know. Let's get everybody in the same room. Usually, you can get an answer in a matter of minutes, whereas you could lose whole days with just back and forth, politicking, and siloing. Yeah, that's exactly. That is the time at which a meeting is cheaper. Yes, right. A meeting is more effective most cases. Meetings just happen because it's like I'm the manager and I believe that, because of the bureaucratic like, you know, motion that I go through that I need to call a meeting or the social aspect of. You know, it's better when we're all together. It's not, right? It's not.
But when it is— to avoid the back and forth— right? The 532 slacks or 19 back-and-forth emails and and looping in other people at different milestones in in the disagreement just get everybody together and say, 'Look, Ryan believes this. I believe this. We cannot get on the same page about what the next step is. Since we were all the stakeholders here, we need some help.' Like, which one? What do we do? Or is it something totally different? But getting that clarity is what keeps that project, that key initiative, whatever, on track. So, we've decided that we're going to call a meeting. It's appropriate to do it. How does it happen? How should the meeting be structured? Yeah. The first thing is just to determine what kind of meeting is, and should it happen in general.
I think we covered that. Correct. Right. So now it's just. what now. If we're gonna do it, how do we make sure this time is spent effectively? Yeah. So, Kind of understanding, back to the book, read this before our next meeting. There's seven principles in the book about kind of the modern meeting. So the modern meeting supports a decision that's already been made. I love what the author says. He says, 'Meetings don't make decisions, leaders do.' That's a big deal because it can be difficult to make a decision by a committee. So I know that that can be confusing. If we agreed together, fine. We all agreed on this thing, but then leaders make decisions. It doesn't mean you black box the decision, but someone has to eventually make a decision.
It's also why, as a leader, so if you're watching this, leader at the scalable company, you should speak last. It's also why, you know... being clear on who is the person who ultimately gets to make the call, right? Because oftentimes not necessarily hierarchical. Sometimes you have a project lead or something. So just being clear and whoever the person who gets to ultimately make the call, not because they're necessarily the best looking or the highest paid, but because they're the ones that are going to bear the burden. You're on the hook. Yeah. You're on the hook being in charge. Speak last, but make the call. Exactly. Make the call. So number one, it needs to support a decision that's already been made. Correct. It moves fast. It ends on schedule.
So that suggests that it actually has a timeline. Yep. Not just a start time, but an end time. Correct. And we'll get to that. It limits the number of attendees. Back to the social aspect, the committee, identifying the stakeholders is critical. Who needs to be here? I think a lot of the times what we'll see is because we are unsure. We'll just invite everybody. Because someone is unsure as to the scope or the impact or the ownership of different projects and key initiatives, they don't want to leave anyone out because they don't want to... I mean, it comes from a good place. Typically, people don't want to miss an opinion or have someone... out of the loop. So the meeting invite list gets gigantic. And that's when the cost goes up.
And if you're invited to a meeting, you kind of feel— like you need to participate. So if you don't reject that invite because you go, look, there's no reason for me to be here. Then now you're there and now you feel the need to participate, have an opinion, even if you don't like it. You don't want to invite me to a meeting if you don't need my opinion. Yeah, we're going to have— I'll have them. You're going to get it whether you like it or not. Right. But that goes both ways, right? So if you're going to invite somebody to a meeting, you need to be clear on, 'we're doing this meeting for this reason.' I need you there because of this reason.
Inverse, like if you get invited to a meeting, you need to ask those questions. You need to ask, 'what's the meeting about?' 'Why do you need me there?' If it's clear, obviously it's clear. But if it's not, make sure you get clear on it. That ownership exists on both sides. So that's number three. So support a meeting that the decision has already been made. Moves fast. Schedule. Limit the number of attendees. What's number four? Rejects the unprepared. Okay. So that's a big deal. All right. So going back to supports a decision that's already been made, it rejects someone who is unprepared. If you don't show up knowing why you're there, what the goal of the meeting is, you haven't done your homework and your prep work. You can't be there.
You got to go. And that's fine. That's fine. It's actually how you keep meetings from becoming informational. So keep going and we'll hit on that. So that's number four. Number five is it produces committed action plans. We are going to leave here with an action plan. We're going to have a meeting. For a stated purpose, uh, once we achieve that, we're going okay, great. So here's what we're going to do. We're going to do this and this and this and this and this, and here's who owns it, and here's when we're going to check back in. That doesn't necessarily mean another meeting. It means an update. It means a status report. A lot of the times, that'll be at one of the already scheduled recurring meetings.
Just to recap: supports a decision, number one; moves fast and ends on schedule, number two. Correct. Limits the number of attendees, number three. Rejects the unprepared, number four. Produces an action plan, which means going into the meeting. You need to know what you want to get out of it. That's number five. Correct. Number six might be my favorite of all. Yes. Refuses to be informational. Yes. Right. Reading memos is mandatory. Right. So, if you're going to reject the unprepared, then there has to be prep work done, right? You have to send out information about why we need a meeting. There are memos, right? There are write-ups. There are just kind of a stated purpose of the meeting and those details that someone would need to show it prepared, right?
Yes, and I love that because, I mean, we have quarterly and, you know, when we do our monthly all-hands and we're revealing, like, here's kind of our... you know, I'm sorry, when we do our monthly All Hands, we talk about... kind of a the monthly business review and we do our quarterly. When we gather quarterly and reveal the new. Here's our quarterly plan. Those are informational, but that's critical because everybody needs to know that stuff. It's here's what we're doing, but it still has an action item, which is, okay, now go and meet with your team and do this. Correct. So there's got to be some type of action, right? Even meetings that are inherently informative. Still have an action element to it. If not, you're just disseminating information.
It could just be an email. All right, so that's number six. What is number seven? Works alongside a culture of brainstorming. Right. So. Ex-understands that we can have kind of these brainstorming meetings and a lot of those are happening at the team level. Right in the B &O list, the bottlenecks and opportunities. If we're meeting weekly as teams, if we're meeting weekly with one-on-ones with your direct supervisor, if we're meeting weekly as a leadership team, if we're meeting monthly as a company and as a leadership team in all hands, and reviewing these things, those are times where brainstorming sessions in the form of bottlenecks and opportunities arise, right? So if we're brainstorming, and especially keeping that backlog, and then quarterly reviewing that backlog to say, 'Is there something that we've already had an idea for?' Would help us achieve this goal faster, right?
Could we do this faster by an idea that we've already had? If you're not backlogging, that doesn't work, right? But brainstorming and backlogging together also is just a list of ways that you can solve problems or capitalize on opportunities that you may not know exist yet. But if you're not doing that, then your meetings, then these action-based meetings are going to be stale, stagnant. They're going to turn into information. So brainstorming sessions versus a meeting, those are two critical distinctions. So brainstorming, typically brainstorming for us happens, again, a lot in those quarterly sprint plans. So enter before. Yeah. And so that's that's the basic structure. We think about meetings like 'doesn't need to exist.' If it does, make sure that that it kind of checks off all seven of those criteria.
All right. If I'm going to request a meeting, I've decided We got to have it. What is that process? What is that flow? Okay, that's good. So... If we've said, 'Here's everything that a modern meeting is. Yeah, this is what the meeting should be. Here's what it looks like. Everybody got it. They're clear on that. It checks one of the appropriate boxes, time to request a meeting.' So the first thing you've got to ask yourself, am I seeking clarity or resolution to conflict about a decision that's already been made? If the modern meeting supports a decision that's already been made, and really it's either about clarity or conflict resolution, then is it one of those things? Or is it one of the other two?
Right that we talked about is there some massive threat or massive opportunity uh that would cause us uh to pull the cord. Right, we need to stop. Acknowledging those are rare in general, it's those should happen. This decision has been made. We've got a lack of clarity or a lack of—of— or we've got an abundance of conflict around this. Check. Need to have a meeting. Step one: What's— what's kind of the next step? Gonna answer the question: Who needs to be there? Right and not just who do I? Who do I want to be there? Like, this. You have to take ownership of a successful and profitable. I think of your meetings in—in, like, an ROI. What does it take to ROI this meeting?
So, if someone doesn't need to be there, that's going to make it, and you invite them, that's going to make it a little bit harder to ROI because the cost goes up with every attendee. So also, back to what we said, you don't want to make someone... feel like, if they sit there and they don't participate, then they're less important, less relevant, less smart, whatever. So if you're inviting people, again, they're going to feel the need to participate. But if they don't actually have a reason to be there, then you shouldn't invite them. So you have to make that like stakeholder list. Yeah. And don't, and don't think about like, oh, if you're worried, you're going to hurt their feelings. This happens a lot.
Like, oh, I feel like that if this person is invited, they're going, they're going to feel excluded. Well, a couple of things. Number one. Most people would love to be in less meetings. Yeah, hurt my feelings all day long. Exactly. But if you're worried about it, go to them and say, 'I'm calling this meeting. I wasn't planning on having you because it didn't seem like you would necessarily go. If you feel like you could add value to it, here's the purpose. Here's why we're meeting. You can come in. But if not, I just want you to know I'm not inviting you because I respect your time.' Who isn't going to appreciate that? What kind of... evil monster would ever speak that kindly to someone. Right. So you hit on a great point.
And again, we haven't requested the meeting yet. We're just walking through the process of how you know if you should. So after you make that list of people—those you believe need to be there, the stakeholders, right? The third thing is you have to write a detailed agenda. Right. So if we said, 'Mm hmm.' The modern meeting supports a decision that's already been made, and the modern meeting is not informational— that the reading of memos is required. Agenda is how you start to craft that memo, right? It's how you start to give people the information to be prepared. And it's also how you start to clarify that problem. clarify that opportunity or that conflict. So you have to write out this agenda that's on you if you're going to call a meeting.
Yep. And generally it's just, you know, here's... here's the chat. here's the situation. this is the challenge. these things need to come up and we're going to talk about this, this, this. and ultimately we want to reach a decision like this. so just getting, it doesn't have to be, yeah, you're writing a book, but you know. there needs to be some clarity around. Why are we doing this? What are we going to accomplish? What are some things that are going to be discussed? Who's going to be there? All that fun stuff. The meeting purpose and what success looks like. That's a big one. If this meeting is successful, here's the purpose. There is conflict around this, or there is a lack of understanding on how to do this.
That is the purpose, right? Here's what success looks like. We leave the meeting with boom, boom, boom, right? One, two, three, very clear. Here's who I believe needs to be there to enable that success. Here's about how long I think we should block out for the meeting. Now. After you put that together, it's up to you to go back and review it. You review it and ask yourself, do I still believe that this requires a meeting? Yep. Right. Do I still believe this requires a meeting? And that, just so we're clear, that's step four. So coming back, step one, am I seeking clarity? That initial check. Two, make the list of the people to invite. Three, create that agenda. Four, after looking at the agenda and the people that don't want to be here, does this really need to happen?
Could this be an email? Right. You know, could I just go and just hit that person up on Slack real quick and find out, like, is there another way? That's step four. Is this my decision to make? Oh, that's a big one. That's another big one, right? Again, there's a lot of people that are just scared to make decisions. Now, I don't mean that you should just take inappropriate ownership of decisions, nor should your people. However, The appropriate ownership of decisions is required. It's required for leadership. It's required for individual contributors. If you've clearly defined something, you understand your role. And you're looking at this and you're just, you're confused. Maybe what you need is you just need to talk it through with someone.
You need to help processing something, but at the end of the day, the decision is yours to make. That is not- time to call a meeting—yes, that's time to to have a conversation with someone. Hey, can I run something by you real quick? Look, when you have some time later on today or tomorrow for me to kick this around. A lot of the times, what you'll find after you've written this agenda and written the purpose and what success looks like is you're just struggling making a decision that's yours to make. That's fine. That's fine. But it doesn't require a meeting in most cases. Right. If something hasn't changed, you're just struggling with a decision. Go and have a one-on-one conversation with someone. That person may not even be within your organization, right?
Depending on the struggle you're having, maybe it's a mentor, maybe it's a peer in another organization, maybe it's an expert or a coach or an industry expert somewhere else that you can talk to and just say, 'Here are the options that I'm torn between. What would you do? Is there a mastermind group that you can go and talk to? This is my problem. This is the decision I'm struggling with. Does anybody have any direct experience in that? It's the way we run our masterminds.' So again, looking through and asking, kind of in step five, as you write out that meeting purpose and what success looks like. Do I still believe that? This requires a meeting. That's almost at every checkpoint. Yeah. Should this happen? That's the nice thing about this.
So going back over the process. Number one. Um, you know, Does this check the box? Should this meeting happen? If yes, who should be there? Then, all right, the dollars can't even be there. What's the agenda? Um, now okay. I've decided this is the agenda. These people here aren't really necessary. That's step four. Step five: write out the meeting purpose and what success looks like. And that's when... you're looking at another good check. But once you've done that, you go: yes, this meeting should exist. Those people should be there. Here's the agenda. This is the purpose. This is the goal. What we're looking to do. What is, what's kind of step six. So now you're going to send that— purpose, goal, agenda— to the people you think should be in the meeting.
Now, you're not gonna request a meeting yet. You're gonna send an email and say, 'We may need to meet.' That would be my subject line of choice— or not sure if we need to meet about this, right? Something that indicates there's a decision needs to be made. I would state the purpose, like what is the problem or opportunity, where do we have conflict, where are we seeking clarity, and say, 'Here's my issue.' Here's kind of what successful resolution of this issue would look like. If we were to have a meeting, here's the agenda that I came up with. Quick question. Does everyone agree that this is a problem and we should probably meet about it? Or... Can someone accomplish that goal? Can someone help me with clarity?
Can someone resolve this conflict without meeting? Yeah, here's what I want everybody to hear with this. The first step is not. Send Google invite. No, that is critical at this company, right? We do not just send blind, uh, Google calendar invites out to folks. All right. First, number one, give it some thought. Should this meeting even happen? Does it check the box that we've already discussed throughout? If so, who needs to be there? Be thoughtful. Be thoughtful. Who should be there? Who maybe doesn't, right? Create that agenda. Step three, detailed agenda. That's going to kind of be a nice check for you. Step four, ask yourself, based on this agenda, Based on the people there, Does it really need to happen? Step five, if yes, write out the meeting purpose, what success looks like.
And then step six, go ahead and send that purpose, the agenda out to all the folks and say, 'Hey. Do we need to meet about this? Then and only then, Step seven, figure out a time, get it on the calendar, send out the calendar invites. So for me, if someone internally or externally requests a meeting, I have a... questionnaire that they get sent a few quick questions before they're sent over to my calendar and one of the last questions is: 'If I could accomplish the goal stated in the purpose, would you still require a meeting? Or would you just want the solution? I'm on the nose with it.' Because protecting that calendar is critical, especially if you have big goals that we've all agreed that we're moving towards in our quarterly sprint planning.
Anything that's going to take us away from that. It better be important or it better be moving that forward. So asking that question is great. What are you really looking for? Are you looking for clarity? Are you looking for resolution? Or are you looking for an audience? Mmm. Do you just need some friends? I mean, do you just need some socialization time or brainstorming? Or are you needing some creative energy? Those are fine. Be self-aware. This process will help you determine which one it is. And again, neither is right or wrong. Abusing meetings is right, so and so at this company. We are not anti-meeting. We are anti-bad meeting and hopefully some of these things will help all of us have more good meetings. Thanks.
